join_order.build: customer(full scan, 1.5M rows, flat_array on c_custkey)

subquery_detail.plan: single_pass_hash_aggregation over all 60M lineitem rows: SUM(l_quantity) grouped by l_orderkey; emit l_orderkey into flat_hash_set where SUM > 300 (scale_factor=1, threshold=300); estimated qualifying orderkeys: ~500K

aggregation_detail.note: Since o_orderkey uniquely identifies each group (one order → one customer, one date, one totalprice), group count equals qualifying orderkey count (~500K); use compact open-addressing hash table keyed by o_orderkey

filter_pushdown.predicate: none (full scan, all rows needed for SUM aggregation)

data_structures.subquery_lineitem_agg: compact_hash_map<int32_t, int64_t>(pre_sized 20M, robin_hood_open_addressing) — maps l_orderkey → SUM(l_quantity); thread-local partial sums merged sequentially; qualifying keys emitted into qualifying_set
data_structures.qualifying_orderkeys_set: flat_hash_set<int32_t>(pre_sized 600K, open_addressing, load_factor=0.7) — used for semi-join probe on both orders and lineitem scans
data_structures.orders_build_hash: compact_hash_map<int32_t, OrderRow>(pre_sized 700K, robin_hood_open_addressing) — keyed by o_orderkey, stores {o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice}; built sequentially from filtered orders (~500K rows)
data_structures.customer_lookup: flat_array<int16_t>(size=1500001, indexed by c_custkey) — direct array lookup for c_name dict code; zero hash overhead since c_custkey is dense 1..1500000
data_structures.aggregation_map: compact_hash_map<int32_t, int64_t>(pre_sized 700K, keyed by o_orderkey) — accumulates SUM(l_quantity) per qualifying order; thread-local maps + merge
data_structures.topk_heap: max_heap<ResultRow, size=100> — maintains top-100 by (o_totalprice DESC, o_orderdate ASC)

execution_phases.name: subquery_lineitem_aggregation
execution_phases.description: Parallel scan of lineitem (60M rows, only l_orderkey + l_quantity columns). Thread-local compact hash maps accumulate SUM(l_quantity) per l_orderkey. After parallel phase, merge thread-local maps into global map and emit qualifying l_orderkey values (SUM > 300, l_quantity scale_factor=1 so threshold=300 raw) into qualifying_orderkeys flat_hash_set.

execution_phases.name: orders_filtered_scan_and_hash_build
execution_phases.description: Parallel scan of orders (15M rows). For each row, probe qualifying_orderkeys set; if hit, collect {o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice} into thread-local vectors. After scan, merge and build compact_hash_map<o_orderkey, OrderRow> (~500K entries).

execution_phases.name: customer_flat_array_build
execution_phases.description: Sequential scan of customer (1.5M rows). Load c_custkey and c_name (dict int16_t). Build flat_array<int16_t>[1500001] mapping c_custkey → c_name dict code. O(1) lookup, no hash overhead.
execution_phases.parallelism: sequential (fast: 1.5M rows × 2 columns, fits in L3)

execution_phases.name: lineitem_main_scan_aggregate
execution_phases.description: Parallel scan of lineitem (60M rows, l_orderkey + l_quantity only). Probe qualifying_orderkeys set; on hit, look up orders_build_hash_map to get {o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice}; look up customer_name_array[o_custkey] for c_name. Accumulate SUM(l_quantity) into thread-local compact_hash_map<int32_t, int64_t> keyed by o_orderkey. After parallel phase, merge thread-local sums.
execution_phases.note: qualifying_orderkeys set and orders_build_hash_map are read-only during this phase — no synchronization needed on lookups; only thread-local agg maps need merging

execution_phases.name: topk_extraction
execution_phases.description: Single pass over aggregation_map (~500K entries). Reconstruct full result row from orders_build_hash_map and customer_name_array. Maintain max-heap of 100 entries ordered by (o_totalprice DESC, o_orderdate ASC). Final heap extraction yields ordered top-100.

parallelism.notes: Phase 1 and Phase 4 each scan all 60M lineitem rows — these are the dominant phases. Phase 2 scans 15M orders rows. All hash table builds on read-only shared structures after construction. Thread-local aggregation maps merged after parallel scan to avoid contention.

index_usage.reason: lineitem and orders are fully scanned for the subquery aggregation and semi-join filter; hash indexes exist but full scans with in-memory hash set probing are faster than index-driven row-by-row lookup at this selectivity

physical_plan.scan_notes: Phase 1 and Phase 4 both scan lineitem; consider a single combined pass reading l_orderkey+l_quantity once: in Phase 1 mode build the agg map; replay from in-memory result for Phase 4. Alternatively, two sequential passes are acceptable since lineitem columns fit in streaming access pattern on HDD with prefetch.
physical_plan.memory_notes: lineitem l_orderkey (60M × 4B = 240MB) + l_quantity (60M × 8B = 480MB) = 720MB total; orders columns ~4 × 15M × 8B = 480MB; customer 1.5M × 4B = 6MB. All fit comfortably in 376GB RAM. L3 cache (44MB) accommodates orders hash map (~500K × 16B = 8MB) and qualifying set (~500K × 8B = 4MB) — hot lookup structures stay in L3.

optimization_notes: Two lineitem scans (phases 1 and 4) dominate; merging into one pass would target ~100ms total
qualifying_orderkeys set (~500K) and orders hash map (~8MB) fit in L3 — probe latency is low
customer flat array lookup is O(1) cache-line access, negligible cost
64-thread parallelism gives ~8-10x speedup over single-threaded on 60M row scans
HDD sequential access is the bandwidth bottleneck; streaming morsel-driven reads minimize seek overhead
